1. Title and Student Information¶

Predictive Maintenance: Machine Failure Prediction¶

Student Name: Ozan Berk Aydin
Student Number: GH1046991
Course: M505 Intro to AI and ML
Submission Date: June 2026

Task: Binary classification; Dataset: Industrial Machine Predictive Maintenance, Kaggle

2. Introduction¶

This project uses sensor, operating and maintenance data to predict failure_within_24h.

Unexpected failures can stop production, increase costs and create safety risks. Earlier warning helps teams plan maintenance before breakdowns.

Each record includes sensor readings, operating conditions, maintenance information and a 24-hour failure label.

3. Data Collection¶

The Industrial Machine Predictive Maintenance dataset was downloaded from Kaggle (2025) and loaded from data/predictive_maintenance_v3.csv.

  • Observations: 24,042
  • Original variables: 15
  • Target: failure_within_24h
  • Source: Kaggle public dataset
  • Usage/licence: Kaggle/uploader terms apply.
  • Repository: https://github.com/ozanberkaydin/M505_Predictive_Maintenance_Project

Pandas supports the tabular analysis (McKinney, 2022).

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

RANDOM_STATE = 42

raw_data= pd.read_csv("data/predictive_maintenance_v3.csv")
df = raw_data.copy()

df.head()

4. Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Initial Observations¶

The dataset contains 24,042 rows and 15 variables. Some sensor fields are missing, so preprocessing is needed before modelling.

In [7]:
df.duplicated().sum()
Out[7]:
np.int64(0)
In [8]:
df["failure_within_24h"].value_counts()
Out[8]:
failure_within_24h
0    20482
1     3560
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [9]:
df["failure_within_24h"].value_counts(normalize=True) * 100
Out[9]:
failure_within_24h
0    85.19258
1    14.80742
Name: proportion, dtype: float64
In [10]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.countplot(data=df, x="failure_within_24h")
plt.title("Target Variable Distribution")
plt.show()

The target is moderately imbalanced, with about 85% normal records and 15% failures within 24 hours. For this reason, F1-score, precision, recall and ROC-AUC are more useful than accuracy alone.

Failure rates are also checked by machine type and operating mode.

In [11]:
for cat in ["machine_type", "operating_mode"]:
    rates = df.groupby(cat)["failure_within_24h"].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)
    print(f"Failure rate by {cat}:")
    print(rates)
    plt.figure(figsize=(6,3))
    rates.plot(kind="bar")
    plt.title(f"Failure Rate by {cat}")
    plt.ylabel("Failure Rate")
    plt.xticks(rotation=15)
    plt.show()

In [12]:
selected_features = [
    "temperature_motor",
    "vibration_rms",
    "pressure_level",
    "rpm"
]

for feature in selected_features:
    plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))

    sns.histplot(
        data=df,
        x=feature,
        hue="failure_within_24h",
        kde=True
    )

    plt.title(f"Distribution of {feature} by Failure Status")
    plt.xlabel(feature)
    plt.ylabel("Count")

    plt.show()

Feature Distributions¶

Failure cases tend to show higher motor temperature and vibration, which supports keeping sensor variables as predictors.

In [13]:
numeric_df = df.select_dtypes(include=["int64", "float64"])

plt.figure(figsize=(12,8))

sns.heatmap(
    numeric_df.corr(),
    annot=True,
    fmt=".2f",
    cmap="coolwarm"
)

plt.title("Correlation Heatmap of Numerical Features")

plt.show()

Correlation Analysis¶

estimated_repair_cost is strongly related to the target, but removed because it is only known after failure. Among usable variables, temperature and vibration show clear positive links with failure risk.

In [14]:
selected_features = [
    "temperature_motor",
    "vibration_rms",
    "pressure_level",
    "rpm"
]

for feature in selected_features:

    plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))

    sns.boxplot(
        data=df,
        x="failure_within_24h",
        y=feature
    )

    plt.title(f"Boxplot of {feature} by Failure Status")

    plt.show()

Outlier Analysis¶

Boxplots show outliers in vibration, temperature and RPM. These may represent abnormal operating states, so they are kept.

Missing Values¶

Missing sensor values are handled inside the pipeline with imputation. This keeps useful rows and reduces leakage risk.

In [15]:
missing_values = df.isnull().sum().sort_values(ascending=False)
missing_values[missing_values > 0]
Out[15]:
vibration_rms        1000
pressure_level        924
temperature_motor     834
current_phase_avg     731
rpm                   533
dtype: int64
In [16]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,4))

missing_values[missing_values > 0].plot(kind="bar")

plt.title("Missing Values by Feature")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.show()

5. Data Cleaning and Feature Engineering¶

Before modelling, leakage fields are removed, missing values are imputed, categories are one-hot encoded and numerical variables are scaled when needed. Leakage uses information unavailable at prediction time and inflates test scores.

In [17]:
X = df.drop(
    [
        "failure_within_24h",
        "failure_type",
        "estimated_repair_cost",
        "timestamp",
        "rul_hours",
        "hours_since_maintenance"
    ],
    axis=1
)

y = df["failure_within_24h"]

Target Leakage Prevention¶

failure_type, estimated_repair_cost, rul_hours and hours_since_maintenance were removed because they may not be available before prediction. They represent post-diagnosis, post-repair, direct RUL or timing-proxy information.

hours_since_maintenance could be valid if it only used past logs, but the reset timing is unclear. I therefore exclude it as a proxy-leakage risk.

In [18]:
X["machine_id"] = X["machine_id"].astype(str)
In [19]:
numeric_features = X.select_dtypes(
    include=["int64", "float64"]
).columns.tolist()

categorical_features = X.select_dtypes(
    include=["object"]
).columns.tolist()

numeric_features, categorical_features
Out[19]:
(['vibration_rms',
  'temperature_motor',
  'current_phase_avg',
  'pressure_level',
  'rpm',
  'ambient_temp'],
 ['machine_id', 'machine_type', 'operating_mode'])
In [20]:
df["machine_id"].nunique()
Out[20]:
20

Feature Selection Decision¶

machine_id has 20 identifiers. I keep it as a categorical feature because repeated observations may capture machine-specific risk patterns without adding a false numeric order.

In [21]:
df["machine_id"].head()
Out[21]:
0    1
1    1
2    1
3    1
4    1
Name: machine_id, dtype: int64
In [22]:
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X,
    y,
    test_size=0.20,
    random_state=RANDOM_STATE,
    stratify=y
)

X_train.shape, X_test.shape
Out[22]:
((19233, 9), (4809, 9))

Train-Test Split¶

An 80/20 train-test split is used with random_state=42. Stratification keeps the failure rate similar in both sets, which is important for this imbalanced target.

Preprocessing Pipeline¶

In [23]:
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectKBest, f_classif
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, GridSearchCV
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, roc_auc_score
In [24]:
numeric_pipeline = Pipeline(
    steps=[
        ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median")),
        ("scaler", StandardScaler())
    ]
)
In [25]:
categorical_pipeline = Pipeline(
    steps=[
        ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="most_frequent")),
        ("encoder", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore"))
    ]
)

Numerical features use median imputation and StandardScaler; categorical features use most-frequent imputation and OneHotEncoder, all inside ColumnTransformer.

In [26]:
preprocessor = ColumnTransformer(
    transformers=[
        ("num", numeric_pipeline, numeric_features),
        ("cat", categorical_pipeline, categorical_features)
    ]
)

6. Data Engineering Comparison¶

Three LR strategies are compared: imputation plus encoding, then scaling, then scaling with SelectKBest.

In [27]:
num_a = Pipeline([("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median"))])

cat_a = Pipeline([("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="most_frequent")),
                  ("encoder", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore"))])

prep_a = ColumnTransformer([("num", num_a, numeric_features),
                            ("cat", cat_a, categorical_features)])

def lr(): return LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)

pipeline_b = Pipeline([("preprocessor", preprocessor), ("model", lr())])

pipeline_c = Pipeline([("preprocessor", preprocessor),
                       ("selector", SelectKBest(score_func=f_classif, k=10)),
                       ("model", lr())])

engineering_pipelines = {
    "A: No Scaling": Pipeline([("preprocessor", prep_a), ("model", lr())]),
    "B: Scaling": pipeline_b,
    "C: Scaling + SelectKBest": pipeline_c
}

eng_results = []
for name, pipe in engineering_pipelines.items():
    pipe.fit(X_train, y_train)
    eng_results.append({"Strategy": name, "F1 Score": f1_score(y_test, pipe.predict(X_test))})

comparison_df = pd.DataFrame(eng_results)
comparison_df
Out[27]:
Strategy F1 Score
0 A: No Scaling 0.747352
1 B: Scaling 0.746970
2 C: Scaling + SelectKBest 0.445305
In [28]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7,4))
sns.barplot(data=comparison_df, x="Strategy", y="F1 Score")
plt.title("Preprocessing Strategy Comparison")
plt.xticks(rotation=15)
plt.show()
In [29]:
feature_to_scale = "temperature_motor"
raw_feature = X_train[[feature_to_scale]]
imputed = SimpleImputer(strategy="median").fit_transform(raw_feature)
scaled = StandardScaler().fit_transform(imputed)
scaling_demo_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Raw": imputed.ravel(),
    "Scaled": scaled.ravel()
})

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9,3))
sns.histplot(scaling_demo_df["Raw"], ax=axes[0], bins=30)
axes[0].set_title("Before Scaling")
sns.histplot(scaling_demo_df["Scaled"], ax=axes[1], bins=30)
axes[1].set_title("After StandardScaler")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Data Engineering Results¶

Scaling did not improve LR in this dataset: F1 stayed at 0.747. SelectKBest reduced F1 to 0.445, so feature selection was not used.

7. Model Training¶

Four scikit-learn classifiers are compared:

  • Logistic Regression: an interpretable baseline.

  • Decision Tree: clear threshold rules, but overfit risk.

  • Random Forest: a more stable tree ensemble.

  • Gradient Boosting: strong, but sensitive to tuning.

In [30]:
models = {
    "Logistic Regression": LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    "Decision Tree": DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    "Random Forest": RandomForestClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    "Gradient Boosting": GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE)
}
In [31]:
model_results = []

for model_name, model in models.items():
    model_pipeline = Pipeline([("preprocessor", preprocessor), ("model", model)])

    cv_scores = cross_val_score(model_pipeline, X_train, y_train, cv=5,
                                scoring="f1", n_jobs=1)

    model_pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
    y_pred = model_pipeline.predict(X_test)
    y_proba = model_pipeline.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

    model_results.append({
        "Model": model_name,
        "CV F1 Mean": cv_scores.mean(),
        "CV F1 Std": cv_scores.std(),
        "Accuracy": accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred),
        "Precision": precision_score(y_test, y_pred),
        "Recall": recall_score(y_test, y_pred),
        "F1 Score": f1_score(y_test, y_pred),
        "ROC-AUC": roc_auc_score(y_test, y_proba)
    })

model_results_df = pd.DataFrame(model_results)
model_results_df.sort_values(by="F1 Score", ascending=False)
Out[31]:
Model CV F1 Mean CV F1 Std Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score ROC-AUC
2 Random Forest 0.881561 0.008188 0.961530 0.876967 0.860955 0.868887 0.990771
1 Decision Tree 0.819643 0.012757 0.945727 0.815385 0.818820 0.817099 0.893301
3 Gradient Boosting 0.819905 0.006951 0.948014 0.853211 0.783708 0.816984 0.980612
0 Logistic Regression 0.749084 0.011855 0.930547 0.810855 0.692416 0.746970 0.961615

Initial Model Comparison¶

Models are compared with 5-fold CV and held-out test scores. Random Forest performs best.

In [32]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))

sns.barplot(
    data=model_results_df.sort_values(
        by="F1 Score",
        ascending=False
    ),
    x="Model",
    y="F1 Score"
)

plt.title("Model Comparison by F1 Score")
plt.xticks(rotation=20)

plt.show()

F1 is the main metric because failures are the minority class.

8. Hyperparameter Tuning¶

Grid search is used to tune LR and RF hyperparameters for F1 and generalisation.

For RF, tree count, depth, split size and max_features control stability, complexity and generalisation.

In [33]:
rf_pipeline = Pipeline(
    steps=[
        ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
        ("model", RandomForestClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
    ]
)
In [34]:
param_grid = {
    "model__n_estimators": [100, 200],
    "model__max_depth": [10, None],
    "model__min_samples_split": [2, 5],
    "model__max_features": ["sqrt", "log2"]
}
In [35]:
lr_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
])

lr_param_grid = {
    "model__C": [0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100],
    "model__penalty": ["l2"],
    "model__solver": ["lbfgs", "liblinear"]
}

lr_grid_search = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=lr_pipeline,
    param_grid=lr_param_grid,
    cv=5,
    scoring="f1",
    n_jobs=1
)

_ = lr_grid_search.fit(X_train, y_train)

lr_grid_search.best_params_, lr_grid_search.best_score_
Out[35]:
({'model__C': 10, 'model__penalty': 'l2', 'model__solver': 'liblinear'},
 np.float64(0.7532451736196693))

Logistic Regression Hyperparameter Tuning¶

C controls regularisation and solver changes optimisation. The grid checks whether weaker regularisation improves recall without overfitting.

In [36]:
grid_search = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=rf_pipeline,
    param_grid=param_grid,
    cv=5,
    scoring="f1",
    n_jobs=1
)

_ = grid_search.fit(X_train, y_train)
In [37]:
grid_search.best_params_, grid_search.best_score_
Out[37]:
({'model__max_depth': None,
  'model__max_features': 'sqrt',
  'model__min_samples_split': 2,
  'model__n_estimators': 200},
 np.float64(0.8823349493056745))
In [38]:
best_rf = grid_search.best_estimator_

y_pred_tuned = best_rf.predict(X_test)
y_proba_tuned = best_rf.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

final_metrics = {
    "Accuracy": accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned),
    "Precision": precision_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned),
    "Recall": recall_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned),
    "F1 Score": f1_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned),
    "ROC-AUC": roc_auc_score(y_test, y_proba_tuned)
}

final_metrics_df = pd.DataFrame([final_metrics])
final_metrics_df
Out[38]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score ROC-AUC
0 0.962362 0.879828 0.863764 0.871722 0.991151
In [39]:
best_rf_model = grid_search.best_estimator_

feature_names = best_rf_model.named_steps["preprocessor"].get_feature_names_out()

importances = best_rf_model.named_steps["model"].feature_importances_

feature_importance_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Feature": feature_names,
    "Importance": importances
}).sort_values(by="Importance", ascending=False)

feature_importance_df.head(15)
Out[39]:
Feature Importance
1 num__temperature_motor 0.225571
4 num__rpm 0.200313
0 num__vibration_rms 0.189203
2 num__current_phase_avg 0.155340
3 num__pressure_level 0.069983
5 num__ambient_temp 0.036796
31 cat__operating_mode_normal 0.021592
32 cat__operating_mode_peak 0.021141
30 cat__operating_mode_idle 0.011002
28 cat__machine_type_Pump 0.007260

7 cat__machine_id_10 0.004724

In [40]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))

sns.barplot(
    data=feature_importance_df.head(15),
    x="Importance",
    y="Feature"
)

plt.title("Top 15 Feature Importances - Random Forest")
plt.xlabel("Importance")
plt.ylabel("Feature")

plt.show()

Feature Importance Analysis¶

Random Forest importances make the model easier to interpret. After excluding leakage and proxy-leakage fields, the strongest predictors are pre-failure sensor variables: temperature, RPM, vibration, current and pressure.

Hyperparameter Tuning Results¶

In [41]:
cv_results = pd.DataFrame(grid_search.cv_results_)

plt.figure(figsize=(7,4))
sns.lineplot(
    data=cv_results,
    x="param_model__n_estimators",
    y="mean_test_score",
    hue="param_model__max_depth",
    marker="o"
)
plt.title("Random Forest Hyperparameter Effect")
plt.ylabel("Mean CV F1 Score")
plt.xlabel("Number of Trees")
plt.show()

After excluding hours_since_maintenance, RF selected 200 trees, no depth limit, split size 2 and max_features='sqrt'. This gave CV F1 = 0.882 and test F1 = 0.872.

Class Imbalance Check¶

Failures are about 15%, so balanced Logistic Regression is tested against regular LR as a sensitivity check. The final Random Forest was not re-tuned with class_weight="balanced"; RF class weighting and threshold tuning are future work.

In [42]:
balanced_lr = Pipeline([("preprocessor", preprocessor),
                        ("model", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000,
                                                     class_weight="balanced",
                                                     random_state=RANDOM_STATE))])

balanced_lr.fit(X_train, y_train)
balanced_pred = balanced_lr.predict(X_test)

scaled_lr = Pipeline([("preprocessor", preprocessor),
                      ("model", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))])

scaled_lr.fit(X_train, y_train)
scaled_lr_pred = scaled_lr.predict(X_test)

imbalance_df = pd.DataFrame([
    {"Model": "Logistic Regression", "F1 Score": f1_score(y_test, scaled_lr_pred)},
    {"Model": "Balanced Logistic Regression", "F1 Score": f1_score(y_test, balanced_pred)}
])
imbalance_df
Out[42]:
Model F1 Score
0 Logistic Regression 0.746970
1 Balanced Logistic Regression 0.755998

Balanced LR gives F1 = 0.756 versus 0.747 for regular LR. This is a small gain, but still below RF, so RF remains the final model.

Loss Function Curve¶

Gradient Boosting training loss is plotted by boosting stage to show how optimisation changes over time.

In [43]:
gbm_loss_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
])

gbm_loss_pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
train_loss = gbm_loss_pipeline.named_steps["model"].train_score_

plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.plot(range(1, len(train_loss) + 1), train_loss, marker="o", markersize=3)
plt.title("Gradient Boosting Training Loss")
plt.xlabel("Boosting Stage")
plt.ylabel("Deviance Loss")
plt.show()

The loss decreases and then flattens, so the final model should be chosen using validation metrics, not training loss alone.

9. Final Evaluation¶

In [44]:
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix

cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_tuned)

plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Blues")
plt.title("Confusion Matrix")
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")
plt.show()

false_negatives = cm[1, 0]
false_positives = cm[0, 1]
print("False negatives:", false_negatives)
print("False positives:", false_positives)

False negatives: 97
False positives: 84

10. Visualization and Discussion¶

Confusion Matrix Interpretation¶

The confusion matrix shows the practical errors (Hunter, 2007): false negatives are missed failures, while false positives are unnecessary checks.

In [45]:
from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve

fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(y_test, y_proba_tuned)

plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f"AUC = {final_metrics['ROC-AUC']:.4f}")
plt.plot([0,1], [0,1], linestyle="--")
plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.title("ROC Curve")
plt.legend()
plt.show()

ROC Curve Interpretation¶

The ROC curve is close to the upper-left corner. After removing hours_since_maintenance, ROC-AUC falls from 0.996 to 0.991. This is still strong, but more defensible because another proxy-leakage risk is removed.

11. Conclusion¶

After removing leakage columns and proxy-risk hours_since_maintenance, the tuned Random Forest is still the strongest model. It reaches test F1 = 0.872 and ROC-AUC = 0.991, compared with F1 = 0.913 and ROC-AUC = 0.996 before removing that proxy.

The key features are temperature, RPM, vibration, current and pressure, which is consistent with stressed machines failing more often.

Limitations¶

This analysis uses one clean, possibly simulated Kaggle dataset, which may explain the high ROC-AUC after leakage removal. Real factory data may include more noise, drift and weaker class separation. Random splitting and repeated test-set choices may overestimate generalisation. Also, one-hot encoded machine_id may let the model learn machine-specific risk patterns, not only sensor relationships. False negatives remain the main operational risk.

Future Work¶

Before deployment, I would pilot the model on recent company data, audit maintenance-log timing, monitor false negatives, drift and calibration, and use human review for high-risk alerts. Future validation should use time- or machine-based splits, a separate validation set or nested CV, plus RF class weighting and threshold tuning.

12. References¶

Kaggle. (2025). Industrial Machine Predictive Maintenance Dataset.

Pedregosa, F. et al. (2011). Scikit-learn: Machine Learning in Python. JMLR, 12.

McKinney, W. (2022). Python for Data Analysis. O'Reilly.

Hunter, J.D. (2007). Matplotlib: A 2D Graphics Environment. CSE, 9(3).
